I am thrilled about this wonderful feature! I was able to spare some time to examine it. I have just provided a few comments and suggestions.

Here is how TORCH_CHECK_* is defined:
```C++

#include <glog/logging.h>

// Additional macros on top of glog
#ifndef NDEBUG
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#else // !NDEBUG
// These versions generate no code in optimized mode.
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) \
  while (false)                    \
  CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#endif // NDEBUG
```


This PR broke FB internals. It seems that the real cause of the problem is the change on the semantics/behaviour of `*CHECK*` on Core. See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/82032#issuecomment-1203695164.

Regardless, to resolve the situation I'm issuing a forward fix at [D38383266](https://www.internalfb.com/diff/D38383266) on Phabricator and at #6357 on Github.
Thanks!

Thank you for your interest in LightGBM! But I am -1 on this change. `withVisible()` adds overhead that is unnecessary to meet the behavior you're trying to achieve.

From `?withVisible`

> This function evaluates an expression, returning it in a two element list containing its value and a flag showing whether it would automatically print.

I'm opposed to adding the overhead of constructing that list when it isn't clear how the behavior as of this pull request would be different than the current behavior of `{lightgbm}`.

`lgb.importance()` already returns a `data.table` of importances without using `invisible()`. If you've found that `data.table`'s default printing behavior is not what you want, you can assign the result of `lgb.importance()` to a variable in your code and call `print()` on it directly.
Seems like an improvement. Thanks. :)

Currently, you are creating an instance within the `spanner_create_database_with_default_leader` and `spanner_update_database_with_default_leader` samples. 

This is an issue as if there is a failure later on in the sample, the instance does not get cleaned up correctly. Additionally the second sample fails as it tried to create the same instance.

The instance creation should be removed from the samples and you should instead be using `sample_instance`, or a pytest fixture that uses `sample_instance`. This will ensure that the instance clean up is handled correctly.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient
Most excellent. Excited to get this in the hands of users.

I noticed a few small things and then one bigger breaking thing caused by running `black` on some files.

_Small things and some nits:_

Since we're updating the `Makefile` I compared it with the python project template `Makefile` and noticed we could add / change a few things:

- `find . -name '__pycache__' -exec rm -rf {} +` at the end of the `clean-pyc` command
- We could split out the `validate-docs` command from there if we wanted to have that separated
- `make notes` is missing "build" from the towncrier line, it should be: `towncrier build --yes --version $(UPCOMING_VERSION)`
- nit cleanup (since both seem to work): `tox elint` -> `tox e lint` in `make lint` command

I also noticed some warnings when running `make notes` related to titles in the docstrings [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L27) and [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L45) that can be silenced by just removing the `-----------` which makes it a title. `black` also seems to be happy with that so this works. Unless we want to keep that formatting in which case adding one more `-` since it is too short reduces the noise from the warning to just an "unexpected title" and it isn't as loud of a warning (not bright red). I'd be good with removing them altogether or extending.

---
_Big thing caused by `black` commit:_

I noticed that `bumpversion` can't find the library version anymore because it annoyingly only looks for single quotes in the line `version='3.0.0'` (in `setup.py`) and we (maybe accidentally?) ran `black` on `setup.py` which changed it to double quotes. We should change it back to single quotes as `version='3.0.0'` and then it does find the version correctly.

We also ran `black` on `docs/conf.py` which changed [this line](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/docs/conf.py#L26-L26) which we will need to change back to `setup_version = line.split("'")[1]` once the above `version` change is made.

Unless there's another way to get around it, but currently those are the changes I made locally that got it all building.

Quick note about overriding:

> I also would have expected _set_and_wait to become part of the Signal class itself so that it could be overridden more easily.

Signal does currently have an overridable `_set_and_wait` method. Somehow I had also missed this previously. The default `Signal._set_and_wait` simply calls `_set_and_wait`, and the `set` method will call `self._set_and_wait` in a thread.

So if we do have a poorly-behaving 2-PV signal as in the AD classes, then we absolutely can make a special handler for it by subclassing `EpicsSignal` and overriding `_set_and_wait`. I've been convinced that the details of this are very application-specific.

I'm generally happy with these changes. I'm keen to note that the new `dask_kubernetes.experimental.KubeCluster` doesn't have to match the API for the existing `dask_kubernetes.KubeCluster`. We are free to deviate however we feel makes sense. It is still undecided if it will replace the existing at some point or just sit along side it, but if it does replace we will just make one breaking change at some point. So don't worry about making large changes here.

It's interesting that you note the existing cluster manager stops you from using secrets in your env vars. Sorry about that! These are things I'm definitely keen to fix in the new implementation.

I want to try and balance user experience with functionality. For folks that just want plain old env vars it sucks to have to do things like `[{"name": "foo", "value": "bar"}]` when dictionaries literally mean this and you could just pass `{"foo": "bar"}`. This is one of my particular grumbles about using `boto3`. It's common to see users write helper functions to convert dicts to/from the list of dict form and they shouldn't have to do that.

However as you rightly point out k8s supports more complex options here, like using secrets. In which case you would need to pass something like `[{"name": "foo",  "valueFrom": {"secretKeyRef": {"name": "mysecret", "key": "foo"}}}]`.

I would be really keen to support both dicts and lists of dicts, and we can just type check and convert dicts to lists of dicts automatically. What do you think?

Thanks for taking this on!  Code LGTM, just some comments on the code comments and tests.  I do think this makes the Time Series visualization more correct, even if it's hard to actually generate a case where it is visually different.

FWIW - I expected to be able to get this to visually look different by changing the demo's `single_value` example to be ten 0.0 values.  This should have triggered the "wrong" treatment (two peaks of count 5, rather than one peak of count 10), because this code would generate 30 intervals from -1.0 to 1.0, and right at the middle, the 0.0 values would fall on the middle boundary and get split 0.5/0.5.

However, that doesn't happen, which I eventually figured out is due to floating point error 🤦‍♂️   The bins generated by the logic in that case actually look like this:

```
0: {x: -1, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
1: {x: -0.9333333333333333, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
...
14: {x: -0.06666666666666665, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 10}
15: {x: 0, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
...
29: ​{x: 0.9333333333333333, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
```

Critically, although the `x` for bin 15 is exactly 0, for bin 14, `x = 0.0666...5` while `dx = 0.0666...7`, so they don't quite add up to `0.0`:

```
>>> -0.06666666666666665 + 0.06666666666666667
1.3877787807814457e-17
```

As a result, because it sees that `0.0 < 1.38777...e-17`, doesn't split them evenly after all (it does technically put them in the "wrong" bin, but visually that's less important).

Anyway, maybe at some point I'll try to fix this by ensuring we actually use the same exact values for left/right edges of adjacent bins, since at least in theory having two different calculations could mean we double-count or incorrectly omit counts.

Hey, thanks a lot for the PR and the time it took to implement this.

I'm going to be blunt and extremely apologetic for a second and say that I'm not going to accept this PR for a few reasons.

1. The scope is way too broad.
2. The development of these features requires a lot of design discussion.
3. The development of these features are already planned and are in the process of being discussed.
4. Current focus is in 1.7 which will release soon, 2.0 development will start right afterwards.

For starters, the change from v7 to v8 is a bit more subtle and it breaks things in ways that haven't been covered here (more on that later in the actual review). For example, certain cache policies break in v8 due to subtle changes in certain gateway events like ``PRESENCE_UPDATE``.

Secondly, the design of slash commands that I have in mind currently requires a Python version bump from 3.5 to something like 3.7 or 3.8 and will hopefully be a rich design that allows for slash commands to be implemented in a natural manner. I'm also planning on splitting it from `discord.Client` so a user who doesn't actually want to use slash commands doesn't have to pay for the cost of the state tracking involved with them.

I'm currently working on a slash command implementation and I need to envision a proper design for them that feels right to use so I haven't been in a rush.

I really do appreciate the time and effort it took to write this though, and if you'd be willing to aid in development in the future please feel free to join the [discord server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) so we can do the required discussion for any future contribution, if you're willing, there. 

If you want to reach out and see how development is going for the slash commands project you can also join and reach out -- I'd be happy to discuss it with you.

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (bxt, ho) and the final shape of actions is (bxt, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. the obs shape in this case should be come (2x20, ho) with bunch of zeros in there. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs). 

Makes sense? I guess what you are testing assumes every sequence is of length 1 (max_rollout_length=1). That's why you don't see any errors.
All I am saying is that the lstm part of this test does not have coverage over an important aspect of LSTM models which is the rollout across time. I would recommend taking out the lstm stuff in forward_train from your test. your rollout test seems correct. 

Do the lstm forward_train in a separate method and PR. I can do that too and you can keep your focus on catalog stuff?

This looks great! The CSS implementation degrades gracefully on IE11 and otherwise the behaviors are quite similar, so I would support migrating to it entirely rather than maintaining two parallel implementations.

Shorter-term the main question is cost/risk of migration – if either are high, we might want to use both at least until 0.15. Longer-term once things are consolidated, we could even move it to the design system as `KTextTruncator`.

> Multiple lines - a long word with no breaks

The behavior for the CSS implementation looks good here. Flagging that this case has been a source of many bugs over the years, and warrants a careful eye:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5468
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5464
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/4419


> There's an issue in IE11 with three ellipsis dots not showing in some situations. It seems to be a font issue.

Would you mind filing a separate issue for this? For some context, here is an overview of the intended behavior:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/packages/kolibri-tools/lib/i18n/fonts.py#L589-L603

and the client-side font loading occurs here:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/kolibri/core/assets/src/utils/setupAndLoadFonts.js

> Regarding a11y ... for most techniques, it says that the whole text would be read. 

I've always assumed that the best behavior would be to only read the visible text: if we've made a design decision to truncate it visually, that should probably apply to audible text too? That said, I don't think that either choice is "wrong" and both will result in a reasonable experience for all users.
Also, improve the wording of the changelog/PR to describe the change, and not speak about unintentional changes. They were very much intentional.

This looks great! One small tweak to the test, but otherwise everything looks perfect.

In terms of the JSON deserialization, the [implementation](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc) is all in RapidJSON. I don't know if you've worked with that library before, but the basic pattern is that you have a handler that moves through each JSON element and delegates parsing to whatever more specialized handler is currently on top of the "stack" of delegator handlers. Typically when you start a new object, you push a specialized handler for that object onto the stack, and when you reach the end of that object, you pop the stack, returning control to the handler for the enclosing object.

So, you'll need to make 2 changes in the xgboost_json code to support dart. The simplest one is just adding `"dart"` as an allowed name [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L337). The more important change will be adding a `GradientBoosterHandler::StartArray` method. When an array is encountered inside a `GradientBoosterHandler` object, you'll want to push a new handler onto the stack which will store the contents of that array to some temporary vector which can then be used to modify the leaf values as you've done with the binary case.

The most analogous example of this in the existing code is [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L405-L408). In this case, all we need to do is store the version information and then check it [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L433). In your case, you may need to think about where to store that vector a bit more in order to access it when the leaf values are constructed (or modify them after parsing is fully complete).

Let me know if that gives you enough to start on it or if you'd like some more detailed help.

This is great, the problem definitely has bit me in the past.

I would suggest to follow @simon-mo's approach though and add this as a linter step. The reason is that it's easy to miss tests that are either not captured by the glob or to forgert to include this in new BUILD files (especially as we may be moving to a more package-like BUILD structure in the future). Also, this test is arguably a linter check, so it would be great to include it there.

My proposal here would be:

1. We add a tag `no_main` for all tests that don't expect the `__name__` part
2. We run a bazel query (something like `bazel query 'kind(py_test.*,  tests(python/...) except attr(tags, "\bno_main\b", python/...))'`) we can parse this to json with yq via ` --output xml | xq .`
3. Extract the files to check and run your test against it.

I don't think we lose any flexibility with the tag but we would make sure that everything declared as a `py_test` will be caught.

What do you think?

#### General remarks about the reported issue/proposed fix:

- The `install_as_egg` interface is a non-public interface used internally by setuptools.
  This function, as the name suggests, is very likely to be deprecated, but is currently being used by `easy_install`.
- `easy_install` despite being deprecated, is still an internal mechanism used by setuptools for editable installs.
- In this context, this PR makes sense, since it can fix potential issues for users using editable installs.

#### Review comments:

- The CI output seems to indicate that this implementation is OS-specific. Is this approach safe to use on Windows environments? What would be the impact for the users?
- The implementation overrides a non-public API of `ZipFile`.
  I understand that the method is not completely private, but this also means that we cannot rely on this approach being stable across future Python versions.
  Is this a well-known approach? Is it documented somewhere or used by other big project in the Python community?
  I wonder if instead we could use the already existing code in: https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/98728b103a5c5940abbc2fab60ccfc5fa265d280/setuptools/archive_util.py#L91

Hey @rjmcginness, i think we need one more round of polishing and then we're good to go.

I took the liberty of correcting the integration tests (which had several problems) and adding the corresponding snapshots.
Just to clarify:
* We typically run newly added integration tests locally first to make sure they don't have any major issues
* `sqs_queue` in the context of a test method is a pytest test fixture which creates a temprary queue, and the `sqs_queue` parameter is simply a string, specifically, the temporary queue's URL. i see now that it can be a bit misleading with the class `SqsQueue`, and that there may be confusion that it's the internal queue object you're being handed.
* The `@pytest.mark.aws_validated` marker indicates that someone has validated this test against AWS, meaning the test was executed against the real remote AWS with an AWS account and the test has passed. This can be done by setting `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD` and running the tests
* snapshot tests are a bit tricky (which is why i thought i would add them to make things easier), but in combination with `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD`, you can set `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1`, which will execute the test against AWS, and also record the response (see the updated snapshot json file), when you remove `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1` again, the test will verify against the recorded AWS response

What's left to do is to fix the error responses according to the snapshots :-) We should see the test results from our CI in about 30 minutes.

Is the expected pattern that users should manually set values via `os.environ`? or is this more an example demonstrating "imagine you had these env vars set through some other means, here's how things would work"?

I think manually setting things in os.environ is a bit scary, as it has global side effects (imagine you have multiple dbt projects that you want to load).

I added to that some choices of exception class: C++ without access to pybind forced us to put a lot of different types of exceptions into only one class, ValueError (`std::invalid_argument` in C++), but some of the ones labeled below should instead be `np.AxisError`. We also want to maintain the distinction between user errors (ValueError, TypeError, `np.AxisError`) and internal programming errors, which are AssertionErrors (RuntimeErrors in Awkward 1, as this is what `std::runtime_error` produced, and that was our closest C++ equivalent).

One thing that I didn't mention below is to add type-tracer tests after each value test, which I also mentioned to @stormiestsin in https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/pull/1135#discussion_r744849527.
